speaker: interviewer
text: --seven of Barbara Traub's interview. And you were at the table, sitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very dangerous situation, fraught with a great deal of emotions. I really didn't know what to do. And we were sort of exchanging glances with the girls. And I was watching this SS. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he turned his back and walked away. And then he left the room, where we were working. At that point, one of the girls sitting next to me says, come on, go. Open it. I reached for it. I opened it. And it was a half a loaf of-- of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I turned to her, and I said, he gave us bread? The war is over. I just said it like that. Clearly this was after April 19, when the Frenchman told us that Roosevelt had died and that we shouldn't worry, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I remember things about this factory, this telephone [INAUDIBLE] place. I remember the big posters. It showed the face of a very Germanic-- it showed a very Germanic face. There's a finger over the lips of the person, like this. And underneath, it said, 'Pst! Feind hort mit,' which means, 'Shh! The enemy eavesdrops'-- all over the factory, posters. Pst! Feind hort mit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I also remember a poster, a very funny-looking poster-- it was-- it was-- it was what I know today were warheads, atomic warheads. 'Vau Zwo.' Vau is the V in German-- Sieg Heil, all right? Sieg Heil, the Hitler salute. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So here is this bread. And I say, girls, the war must be over. It was a Saturday. Normally, they kept us working until early in the afternoon, a couple of hours earlier than the other days. But this Saturday-- and I don't have to tell you how we devoured that bread. Or maybe I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- the officer in blue heard a whistle. And we looked up. And the sun-- you know-- I mean, you knew when it was noon because that was around the time for a break. It wasn't noon yet. And sure enough, she put us in-- in a column and marched us back to the camp. It was very strange because she forgot to make sure that the girl with the sign 'Juden kommen,' and the bell-- she didn't make sure that she was walking. Or at least, I don't remember anymore. But it was a very hastily pulled together group of women-- I mean, the column of women-- marching back to the-- to the camp. And in the camp, it's bedlam, SS running back and forth, whistles blowing. The-- the SS guards-- you see, the camp, I should explain, was in a sort of an elevation, barbed wired. And inside were the various buildings where we were housed, and-- and the kitchen. And then there were the very nice quarters for SS and the camp commandant. And the ordinary guards, the ordinary SS guards, are going. And we look down. And we see them go, go. We didn't know what was happening. And there's an exodus of the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next thing, the commandant blows the whistle, Zahlappell. Should we go for Zahlappell? Shouldn't we go for Zahlappell? Are they going to mow us down? What's going on? Well, he keeps blowing the whistle. Nobody runs, as we usually did run. Some go out. And some don't. My Lager sister and I did not go out. Do something, something-- you know, what can you do to me? Something was going on. We didn't know what.

speaker: interviewee
text: It turned out that those who stayed went and-- and stayed in some sort of a formation, heard what the commandant had to say. And what the commandant had to say is, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you are now free. The Russian Armies are very close by. And we are leaving. All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, the whole thing made sense-- the last-minute bread, the stopping work at a certain hour. And at that point, one of the girls who did go out to-- to stand in Zahlappell came back and informed us what the commandant had to say. And with this, the commandant departed. And we all ran out. And we were watching this exodus of these SS, the collar of their gray coats up. You know, they suddenly looked dwarfs, running. And, you know, all we did was yell at them. From the top there, we were still in the-- behind barbed wires. And we yelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you yell?

speaker: interviewee
text: We yelled curse words, God knows-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] 'Verfluchte SS.' This is what I kept yelling, 'verfluchte SS, verfluchte SS.' But that's all we did. And that was so pathetic in a way. I don't know what else we could have done. Today, I can tell you what we could have done. As we found out later, there was a building where the-- where the SS housed its ammunition. There were guns. There were revolvers. We could have gone there and-- and do something, but we didn't. And I wondered why we didn't. Some years back, a woman-- I was writing a book on the Partisan Resistance. And I put in an ad on-- in the papers asking for anybody who has been involved in the resistance-- the Jewish Resistance Partisan Movement-- should contact me. And this wonderful woman contacted me. And she was partly involved. And she told me what I wanted to know. But she also sent me a book of her poems. And she describes-- and she-- she was in the Vilna Ghetto with her mother. And she describes a very similar scene, that they were liberated, and-- I mean, this is so many years later. So it's liberation. And she's there with her mother. And the SS are being driven by-- by the Russian soldiers now. And the Russian soldiers, they didn't go for too many niceties. They didn't wait for Nuremberg tribunals. They did what they had to do whenever they had to do it. And if former victims of Nazi persecution would do something to them, he would let them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this girl, the writer of this poem, wants to go and do something to the SS. And the poem says that the mother put her hand on the daughter and said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, 'For us Jews, it's not the right thing to do to take revenge.' I look back at-- at-- the poem is always associated with my standing on-- on that hill behind barbed wire, yelling at the SS, 'verfluchte SS.' That's all I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Cursed SS.' 'Verfluchte' means cursed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing that happened when all the SS were gone, everybody descended on the kitchen. And there were women eating marmalade with onions, sugar with-- I mean, it was just-- just incredible because the first thing was food. The next thing, in the afternoon, about four Frenchmen in a truck came over to the camp. And you see, by this time, there was nobody around. The camp gates were open. And they said that yes, the Russians are indeed approaching. As it turns out, this was May 6. I mean, my Lager-- my camp sister says it was May 7. I think it was May 6. But we both agree it was Saturday. And that Monday eventually came the-- the final liberation. The Frenchmen said the following, the Russians are approaching. There are rumors that they are raping. And this is a women's camp. We are in the village of Weisswasser. We have our quarters there. Because the camp where the women were, the slave labor camp, were-- was outside the village on that hill. And they said, we offer you asylum. Come, stay with us. We are all men. You will have your own quarters. Stay with us. Just about four of us went-- my camp sister, I, and two other women. The rest didn't want to go. We went. And I remember that-- that night we were up. Nobody slept. The Frenchmen were singing. And they were getting packages from home because they were so-called volunteer workers. And they were singing. And-- and-- and it was just-- nobody could sleep. Nobody could sleep. This is what I remembered. And-- and there were [? bancs ?] there with linen. I haven't seen a pillow with a pillowcase or a bed sheet in a year. And there it was. And it was curtained off. And my camp sister could-- had the same thing. And I couldn't sleep. I couldn't sleep. It's-- it's over. It's over. It's over. It-- it-- it-- And I didn't-- I didn't want to sleep that night. And yet, I wanted to sleep. I just wanted to understand how it was now. It was over. I won't have to look at an SS anymore. And next morning, we were told the Russians are coming close. And we all-- it was May 6, 7-- no, 7 or 8-- because 6 it wasn't-- 7 or 8. We went out on the highway with the Frenchmen and the four women. And a strange thing happened. It was the most beautiful morning. And it reminded me of the morning that they took us from Sighet, from the ghetto, to the synagogue and from there to the train station, and how beautiful Sighet was then, and how I thought I smelled the lilac trees in my mother's garden. And here was a similarly beautiful, beautiful May morning. And we were waiting. It was still a little nippy because we came out 6 o'clock. And we were huddling. And I remembered one of the Frenchmen gave me his jacket, which I kept for many years, many years. And it was a lapin, a rabbit jacket, because it was chilly, and he gave me his jacket. And we were waiting for the Russians. And then, from afar, we saw tanks rolling in. And as they passed by, several things happened simultaneously. One, as they were watching-- they-- they were throwing cigarettes. Then, from the tank turrets, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sleepy eyes, you know, they were sort of rubbing their eyes, were looking and throwing the cigarettes. And the Frenchmen burst out into 'The Marseillaise.' And we all sang 'The Marseillaise.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think it was the height and the end of my idealism because that moment-- and this is very clear in my mind what I thought as the Russian tanks were liberating us, the Frenchmen were singing 'The Marseillaise.' May was beautiful, pure, clean. The sky was blue. The breeze was-- was like silk. And no more SS. And I remember thinking, it's going to be now a different world. All the bad that happened will never again be. Now it's going to be truly a new world, a good world, a clean world, no prejudice-- freedom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought it's just going to be that way. I mean, it was a plateau that is the more painful to remember because it was an illusion. But that's what I thought. And that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that it was the end and the beginning. At the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: The end and the beginning of my idealism.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right at the beginning-- I mean, the height of my idealism, not the end, the height of my believing in the possibility of man-- of-- of undergoing some sort of a psychological mutation through which prejudice, bias is going to be obliterated. I really believed it. That is going to be the beginning of a new world, of a good world, where people can live side by side, being what they want to be without being afraid. I really believed it. And I will never again have that-- I was high, high on idealism. It was just incredible that morning, just incredible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, then what happened, sure enough, as we found out later, the Russians did go into the camp and did rape. And even though the women said, [RUSSIAN], which in Russian means 'Jewish'-- I mean, that's what I heard, particularly one woman was raped. That's what I heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened to the four of us, the Frenchmen said, let's-- well, what are you going to do? Well, we didn't know what to do. The war was over. What do you do? We didn't even know where we were. So one of the Frenchmen suggested, why don't we go in the village and see if we can find some home because the Germans and the Nazis who lived there absconded? It turned out when we were seeing all those Nazi personnel running, their heads, you know, in-- hidden, and we were looking down, they were running to the American-- to the Americans to be-- and-- and they surrendering to the Americans because they didn't want to be caught by the Russians. They knew what-- what would have happened to them with the Russians. That's why they were running. So-- How was it for you to hear what was going on the camps? How were you hearing it? And how is that affecting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affects me. It affects me. And it-- I'm very-- I am very torn about it because raping occurs with every army. The Americans raped. The Nazis raped. I don't have to-- to go into more detail, but it-- and the Russians raped. There is no question about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet, they liberated me. You see the conflict? And yet, they liberated me. They liberated me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also know, for instance, that not because they were Jew-lovers, not because Stalin was a great friend of the Jews, but certain things happened that so many Russian Jews were saved, Russian Jews who were not living under Nazi occupation. I'm talking about the part of Russia that was not saved-- I mean, that was not occupied. Because when-- when the Nazis began to push into Russia after [? Barbarosa, ?] after they declared war, Stalin give a command to leave. He withdrew his people and put them into-- around the Urals. And there were many Jews who were thus saved. So I am very conflicted about that. I am very conflicted. I feel terrible about it. And yet, they were to me the liberators. And so we decided to go with the Frenchmen and look for something where we can recuperate, recover, decide what to do next, until we found a very charming little cottage. There were no inhabitants. Clearly, the Nazis cleaned out. And there were some linen there, I remember. And there was also a goat tethered to the-- to a pole. And one of the Frenchmen said, oh, terrific. I know, you know, the milk. I'll milk the goat. Well, that goat gave us so much trouble because nobody really knew what to do with the goat. And we let the goat go. But the goat kept coming back. It was really a mixed blessing. And it was a very charming house. And there were three Frenchmen and the four of us. We all slept in one room. And the three Frenchmen went around and got food. And one of the women was a very good cook. And one of-- they-- they found some flowers. And I don't know what else, what other ingredients the Frenchmen brought. And she said she will bake a cake. Well, she baked the cake. But something was wrong with that oven because the cake was so hard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I ate it. And I broke two of my teeth, which wasn't difficult because it wasn't so much the cake. But it was the first hard food that I had in long-- in a long time. And because of the lack of vitamins and whatnot, I mean, it was brittle. My teeth were brittle. So I broke my teeth, two teeth. We stayed together with these three Frenchmen, who were awfully decent. And they kept saying to us-- my Lager sister-- my camp sister-- and I decided we have to go back. She had her boyfriend whom she eventually married. And I had a little boyfriend. And it wasn't a boyfriend. I knew about my mother. I knew about my sister-in-law and the little children. I didn't want to know about my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I certainly expected that both my brothers would be alive. And the only point of reference was home, home, home. And so my camp sister and I decided that we want to go back home to Sighet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go there, do you-- do you remember any of the names of the Frenchmen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to, as I talk to you. The-- the-- the-- the Frenchmen who-- one was Edward. And one was Jules, a tall man, yes. Jules-- he wore glasses. He must've been in his 40s. And the little Frenchman who gave me his lapin jacket, his-- his little fur jacket, which I kept for many, many years, his name I-- I don't remember. It may come back to me because he had a very similar name of one of the French Republic's presidents after the war. Rimbaud-- but Rimbaud is the name of a poet, so it couldn't be Rimbaud. I don't remember. But I remember Edward and Jules.

speaker: interviewer
text: No last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, neither did they have last names. So my camp sister, whose name is Dora, and I decided to try to go east. The other two women decided to go west. They eventually, as I learned later, ended up in Sweden. And one of them is still in Sweden. And one of them is here. Now the trek back to Sighet lasted weeks, weeks. Because we were liberated in May, and we stayed for about a week in this little cottage. And then I know it must have been June by the time we got back to Sighet because we got in-- onto one train-- nobody knew where it was going. But after two days, we found ourselves back where we started from. See, it was right after the war. It was-- Russia-- I mean, the Russians occupied that part of Czechoslovakia. There was no organization. Trains were running-- running haphazardly. He who pushed got onto the train. He who didn't was left behind. And so it took us weeks to eventually get to Budapest. And there, I had a very interesting-- I remember. I'm going to tell you this. We got to Budapest. And-- the-- we-- we got off the train. It was daytime. And there were standing the farmer women, the peasant women, with bread and eggs and produce to sell. And there are some Russian soldiers on the platform walking. I don't know what the purpose was. But they did not seem to me as being part of-- of any particular unit. They were just hanging around.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got down. And by that time, I had a little-- I don't know what to call it. It was really a big handkerchief. And I made it into some sort of a-- a bag where I kept God knows what, some-- some rags, probably. What did I have? And my camp sister and I got off the train. And there is that woman. And she pegged us right away, who we were, because she called us-- oh, I'm so glad. Aren't you Jewish? Yes, we said, we are. Well, welcome back, said she. What can I sell you? This [INAUDIBLE]. And there is this warm bread. And she had a bottle of milk. And so we said we would like the milk and-- well, what do you have to give me? My camp sister said, well, I have this handkerchief, you know, this kerchief. Well, she says to me, what do you have there in your satchel? Satchel means that sort of a bag. Yes, it's a bag.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I open it-- because I had really nothing-- I said, I have nothing. She says, I'm not giving it to you. Forget it. Get lost. You know, suddenly we were not [INAUDIBLE]. Apparently, that Russian soldier was watching what was going on. He didn't know whether we were Jews or not. But he did see that we were clearly war refugees. And this peasant woman is trying to sell us food, wanting something. He went over. And he said, [RUSSIAN], grabbed everything from her, gave it to us, and said to her something in Russian which probably meant get lost. She ran like nothing. So I am conflicted, you see. I am conflicted when it comes to that. Eventually we got to Joint Distribution Committee, which was set up by the American Joint Distribution Committee. And from afar, my sister-- my camp sister and I see this gentleman standing in front-- It was a school converted by the Joint to a reception area for refugees. And I see that a gentleman surrounded-- a man surrounded by women and distributing little drinks, schnapps. And as I approach, I say, [HUNGARIAN]. He was one of my uncles who succeeded to hide with false papers and whatnot. And when the war was over and Joint set up this center, he went there every day, waiting for his family-- which his wife and two daughters did come back, my two cousins, but not his son-- and at the same time, giving the drink to every new refugee. Well, he immediately said, I really don't want you to stay here. And he took us to-- no, actually he let-- he let us stay for a night or so. He said, I will see to it that you get home. But if it takes a little long time, then I will get you some room where you could stay, which he did. And eventually, we got back-- no, what happened here was the following. My camp sister heard that her childhood boyfriend was back home. And she didn't want to wait until my uncle, whose name was [? Gigot ?] [? Sigazmund, ?] was going to arrange transportation. She was going to go by foot, by train, by cart, whichever way, she was going to go back to Sighet. And she did find her boyfriend, got married. And today she's the grandmother of several grandchildren. I, on the other hand, did go with my uncle. And until he did arrange transportation for me in a Russian truck with some other people who did go back to Sighet. And I got back to Sighet. And that was a very painful encounter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we have to change tapes.
